paper_metadata.title: French Attitudes on Climate Change, Carbon Taxation and other Climate Policies
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Representative French survey of 3,002 respondents (Bilendi), Feb–Mar 2019.
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Studies French approval of a proposed +50€/tCO2 carbon tax (“Tax & Dividend”: revenue returned lump-sum to adults) and explores acceptance/determinants for diesel and kerosene fuel taxes.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: overestimation of its impact on one’s purchasing power
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Misperceived (overestimated) purchasing power losses from the carbon tax
determinants.definition: Belief that the Tax & Dividend would reduce one’s household purchasing power (even when expected exposure is low).
determinants.mechanism: Perceived personal net losses make the carbon tax seem unfair and not worth the cost, reducing acceptance.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend: +50€/tCO2 carbon tax increase; extra revenue redistributed lump-sum to all adults.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
determinants.evidence_quote: erroneous perceptions ... overestimation of its impact on one’s purchasing power. For instance, 30% ... believe their household would lose
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 30% of people using neither gas nor domestic fuel still think their household would lose purchasing power.
determinants.notes: Presented as an explanation for rejection; no direct regression coefficient shown in this excerpt.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: without mentioning the impact on prices
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Low cost salience (no price-impacts mentioned) increases carbon tax approval
determinants.definition: Approval is higher when the survey omits the carbon tax’s price-increase impacts.
determinants.mechanism: Less salience of immediate price costs increases perceived net benefits, raising approval.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend: revenue returned to all households; alternative wording either omits or mentions the impact on prices.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
determinants.evidence_quote: without mentioning the impact on prices ... higher approval rate of 37%
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Approval rises to 37% when price impacts are not mentioned (vs 10% in the main framing).
determinants.notes: Within-questionnaire framing experiment; cost-salience vs benefits-only wording.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived rural distributional harm from the carbon tax
determinants.definition: Belief that Tax & Dividend would penalize rural households.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived geographic unfairness lowers willingness to accept the carbon tax increase.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) described to respondents with sectoral price impacts salient.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
determinants.evidence_quote: the tax would penalize rural households (47%)
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 47% cite rural penalties as a main problem with Tax & Dividend.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Suspicion that the carbon tax is a pretext to raise taxes
determinants.definition: Belief that the carbon tax policy is mainly intended to raise taxes rather than reduce pollution.
determinants.mechanism: Low trust in tax motives increases perceived unfairness and reduces acceptance.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) and its perceived problems.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
determinants.evidence_quote: policy is simply a pretext to increase taxes (43%)
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 43% cite 'pretext for raising taxes' as a main problem.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Belief that the carbon tax is ineffective
determinants.definition: Belief that Tax & Dividend would not effectively reduce pollution.
determinants.mechanism: Low perceived effectiveness makes the policy seem not worth the costs, reducing acceptance.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) described to respondents.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
determinants.evidence_quote: ineffective to reduce pollution (37%)
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 37% list ineffectiveness for pollution reduction as a problem.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: alternatives are insufficient or too expensive
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived lack of affordable alternatives to fossil fuels
determinants.definition: Belief that households cannot substitute away from fossil fuels because alternatives are insufficient or too expensive.
determinants.mechanism: If substitution seems infeasible, people expect low emissions reduction and view the tax as unfair, reducing acceptance.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) and perceived barriers.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
determinants.evidence_quote: perceived lack of alternatives, seen as insufficient or too expensive (31%)
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 31% cite lack of alternatives (insufficient/too expensive) as a key problem.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Earmarking carbon tax revenue to green/energy-transition investments
determinants.definition: Carbon tax approval is higher when its revenue finances energy-transition and green investments (earmarking).
determinants.mechanism: Green earmarking signals effectiveness and funds alternatives, increasing perceived net benefits and credibility of carbon pricing.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax (+50€/tCO2) with revenue recycled to different uses (revenue recycling acceptance question).
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of carbon tax by revenue-recycling use
determinants.evidence_quote: Overall, the preferred revenue recyclings are investments in the energy transition.
determinants.notes: Supported by discussion that people see carbon taxation as effective only when financing green investments.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: targeted ... constrained with respect to their consumption of petroleum products (fifth with 55% approval)
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Targeted transfer for petroleum-constrained households increases acceptance
determinants.definition: Using carbon tax revenue for a lump-sum transfer targeted to households constrained in petroleum consumption is more acceptable than flat transfers.
determinants.mechanism: Compensation aligns with perceived exposure and facilitates substitution away from petroleum-intensive consumption.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax revenue recycling options including targeted lump-sum compensation to constrained households.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of carbon tax revenue recycling to transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: preferred “lump-sum” transfer ... targeted to people constrained with respect to their consumption of petroleum products (fifth with 55% approval).

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: reduction in the VAT rate (61% approval)
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Use carbon tax revenue to reduce VAT increases acceptance
determinants.definition: Carbon tax revenue used to reduce the VAT rate leads to higher acceptance.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived purchasing-power gains and improved fairness (offsetting regressive VAT) raise support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax revenue recycling options including VAT reduction.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of carbon tax revenue recycling
determinants.evidence_quote: second preferred mechanism for revenue recycling is a reduction in the VAT rate (61% approval).

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Climate-change knowledge
determinants.definition: Higher index of knowledge about climate change (anthropogenic origin, factors, and emissions target).
determinants.mechanism: More knowledge is associated with higher concern and greater willingness to support carbon tax policies.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2 carbon tax; revenue returned lump-sum to adults).
determinants.outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend
determinants.evidence_quote: Knowledge about CC 0.048*** (0.009)

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived catastrophic severity of climate change ('disastrous')
determinants.definition: Belief that climate change impacts would be 'disastrous' (lifestyles largely altered).
determinants.mechanism: Greater perceived severity increases perceived urgency and support for carbon taxation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2 carbon tax).
determinants.outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend
determinants.evidence_quote: CC is disastrous 0.037*** (0.018)
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Positive and significant in column (2); coefficient 0.037***.
determinants.notes: Not significant in column (1) but significant in column (2).

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Ecologist self-identification
determinants.definition: Respondent identifies as an ecologist (political identity in the survey).
determinants.mechanism: Value alignment with environmental priorities increases willingness to accept carbon taxes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2 carbon tax).
determinants.outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Support for the Yellow Vests movement
determinants.definition: Respondent reports supporting the Yellow Vests movement.
determinants.mechanism: Political alignment with Yellow Vests is associated with lower acceptance of carbon tax increases (likely distrust/objections).
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2 carbon tax).
determinants.outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend
determinants.notes: Other Yellow Vests modalities (e.g., understands, is part) are also negative in Table II.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Being 65+ decreases acceptance
determinants.definition: Respondent is 65 years old or older (relative to 18–24 omitted group).
determinants.mechanism: Older respondents are less supportive of the carbon tax increase, holding other factors constant.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2).
determinants.outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend
determinants.notes: Age coefficients are negative for all older brackets in column (2).

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Male gender decreases acceptance
determinants.definition: Respondent is male (relative to female).
determinants.mechanism: Men are less favorable toward Tax & Dividend even though they may have higher climate knowledge.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2).
determinants.outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Living in larger towns increases acceptance
determinants.definition: Higher town-size category (1–5).
determinants.mechanism: Urban residence is linked to higher support for the carbon tax increase (likely better alternatives to car use).
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2).
determinants.outcome_variable: Acceptance of Tax & dividend
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: +0.033*** (column 2) per unit increase in town size category.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Diesel vehicle ownership increases opposition to diesel tax
determinants.definition: Households owning a diesel vehicle.
determinants.mechanism: Greater expected cost exposure from a diesel tax increase leads to opposition.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Increase diesel tax to catch up gasoline taxation level.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of a catching-up of the diesel tax
determinants.evidence_quote: Among the 57% of households who own a diesel vehicle, the opposition augments to 80%.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Opposition is 80% among diesel-vehicle owners (vs 59% overall).

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: 73% of rural households would be opposed
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Rural residence increases opposition to diesel tax
determinants.definition: Rural households vs Paris agglomeration regarding opposition to raising diesel taxes.
determinants.mechanism: Rural car dependence and fewer substitutes raise expected burdens from diesel taxation, lowering support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Increase diesel tax to catch up with that of gasoline.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of a catching-up of the diesel tax
determinants.evidence_quote: 73% of rural households would be opposed, vs. only 40% of those living in the Paris agglomeration.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: broadly perceived effectiveness ... distributive effect ... richer people fly more
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived effectiveness and progressivity explain support for kerosene tax
determinants.definition: Support for a kerosene (aviation) tax is linked to perceived emission-reduction effectiveness and to its distributive effect (richer fly more).
determinants.mechanism: If the tax is viewed as effective and relatively progressive, acceptance increases.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Tax on kerosene (aviation).
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of a tax on kerosene (aviation)
determinants.evidence_quote: main rationale could be ... perceived effectiveness ... and the distributive effect ... since richer people fly more.
determinants.notes: Rationale is proposed by authors; no direct statistical test of this mechanism is shown in the excerpt.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Higher political interest increases Tax & dividend approval
determinants.definition: More interest in politics is associated with greater approval of the Tax & Dividend carbon tax increase.
determinants.mechanism: Engagement with politics may increase attention to climate policy objectives and evaluation, raising support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) with lump-sum revenue to adults.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & dividend
determinants.evidence_quote: Interest in politics (0 to 2) 0.034*** (Table II, column 2)

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: More frequent public transit use increases Tax & dividend approval
determinants.definition: Higher frequency of public transit is associated with higher approval of the Tax & Dividend.
determinants.mechanism: More frequent transit use can provide substitutes for car use, reducing perceived cost exposure from fuel-price increases.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Acceptance of Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) carbon tax increase.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & dividend
determinants.evidence_quote: Frequency of public transit 0.014** (Table II, column 2)

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Belief the carbon tax would penalize the poorest reduces approval
determinants.definition: Respondents cite that the Tax & Dividend would penalize the poorest households.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived regressive/distributive harm lowers willingness to accept the carbon tax increase.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Tax & Dividend (+50€/tCO2) perceived problems; sectoral price impacts salient.
determinants.outcome_variable: Approval of Tax & Dividend (carbon tax acceptance)
determinants.evidence_quote: threat that the tax could penalize the poorest (sixth position with 29%)
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 29% cite poorest penalty as a problem.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Diesel/kerosene determinants are mostly descriptive, not regression coefficients
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: The paper reports strongest divides for diesel tax (vehicle ownership, rural vs Paris) as descriptive comparisons; detailed coefficients are referenced as being in online Appendix.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Kerosene tax rationale is not directly estimated
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: The 'main rationale could be' explanation (effectiveness + distributive fairness) is not quantified as a measured determinant in the shown results.
